text: The surgeon performs an exifopubic median laparotomy, providing a comprehensive view of the abdominal cavity for lesion control.

The surgeon performs primary packing of the abdominal cavity, starting with the most critical bleeder and proceeding in a clockwise manner through the left hypochondrium, right hypochondrium, left lumbar region, left iliac fossa, pelvis, right iliac fossa, right lumbar region, and umbilical area.
The surgeon exposes the spleen and carefully clamps the splenic hilum to avoid damaging the caudopancreatus. The splenocolic ligament is divided, providing access to the posterior side of the spleen.
The surgeon clamps the short vessels around the spleen, ligates them, and divides them in stages. Following this, the splenic hilum is ligated and sectioned.

The surgeon examines the complete abdominal cavity, including the omental bursa, systematically inspecting the small bowel, colon, retroperitoneum, and diaphragm for any lesions. The hemostasis is checked to ensure it remains under control.
A radon's drain is placed in the left hypochondrium near the caudal pancreas to facilitate drainage of the splenic compartment and guide any potential iatrogenic fistula.

The surgeon thoroughly cleanses the abdominal cavity using physiological serum heated to forty degrees Celsius, ensuring the surgical field is free from contamination and aiding in the prevention of hypothermia.
